sections.heading: RT
sections.content:
RT is the primary treatment for solitary plasmacytomas and is used palliatively in MM for pain, impending fractures, or cord compression. It can be part of multimodality therapy for CNS involvement.

Principles:
- RT is the intervention of choice for solitary plasmacytoma, providing excellent local control [MYEL-D].
- For MM, RT is primarily used for palliation of symptoms like pain, cord compression, or impending fracture [MYEL-D].
- Treatment planning should use modern principles: imaging-based delineation of GTV, CTV, PTV, and OARs. CTV expansions should include at least 0.5 cm margin for microscopic extent [MYEL-D].
- Advanced technology (IMRT, VMAT, protons) should be used to limit doses to OARs. Principles of involved-site RT (ISRT) should be followed [MYEL-D].
- Systemic therapy should not be delayed for RT; concurrent therapy can be used with careful monitoring for toxicity [MYEL-D].

Dose Frameworks:
- Solitary plasmacytoma: total dose 40-50 Gy, dose per fraction 1.8-2.0 Gy, fractions 20-25, schedule daily fractions 5 days/week, indication Primary treatment for solitary plasmacytoma (osseous or extramedullary) [MYEL-2, MYEL-D].
- Solitary plasmacytoma (<5 cm): total dose 35-40 Gy, dose per fraction 1.8-2.0 Gy, fractions 19-22, schedule daily fractions 5 days/week, indication Alternative for small plasmacytomas due to high local control rates [MYEL-D].

Approaches:
- Palliative RT for MM: dose fractionation 8 Gy x 1 fraction or 20-30 Gy in 5-15 fractions, concurrent chemotherapy Systemic therapy can be given concurrently; monitor for additive toxicity [MYEL-D]., indication Uncontrolled pain, impending pathologic fracture, or impending cord compression [MYEL-D]., key trial Not specified; based on panel consensus and retrospective data., toxicities Fatigue, myelosuppression, nausea, site-specific effects (e.g., dermatitis, esophagitis) [MYEL-D].
- Craniospinal irradiation (CSI) for CNS disease: dose fractionation Variable; low-dose WBRT ≤23.4 Gy in 13 fractions may be used, concurrent chemotherapy May be combined with intrathecal chemotherapy and systemic therapy; avoid concurrent IT methotrexate and RT within 2 weeks [MYEL-H]., indication CNS involvement with negative or controlled extra-CNS disease; as bridge to consolidation therapy [MYEL-H]., key trial Not specified; based on panel consensus., toxicities Myelosuppression, neurocognitive effects, nausea [MYEL-H].
- Focal RT for CNS lesions: dose fractionation 30 Gy in 10 fractions or similar palliative regimen, concurrent chemotherapy Systemic therapy and intrathecal chemotherapy as per multimodality approach [MYEL-H]., indication Focal neurologic deficits from localized brain, spine, or cranial nerve disease [MYEL-H]., key trial Not specified; based on panel consensus., toxicities Focal edema, necrosis, cognitive changes [MYEL-H].

timeline.title: Solitary Plasmacytoma Regimen
timeline.body: Total dose 40-50 Gy in 20-25 fractions of 1.8-2.0 Gy daily, 5 days/week. Indicated for primary treatment of solitary plasmacytoma.

timeline.title: Solitary Plasmacytoma (<5 cm) Regimen
timeline.body: Total dose 35-40 Gy in 19-22 fractions of 1.8-2.0 Gy daily, 5 days/week. Alternative for small plasmacytomas.

timeline.title: Palliative RT for MM
timeline.body: 8 Gy x 1 fraction or 20-30 Gy in 5-15 fractions. Indicated for uncontrolled pain, impending fracture, or cord compression. Can be given with concurrent systemic therapy.

timeline.title: CSI for CNS Disease
timeline.body: Low-dose WBRT ≤23.4 Gy in 13 fractions (variable). Bridge to consolidation for CNS involvement with controlled extra-CNS disease. Combine with intrathecal/systemic therapy; avoid concurrent IT methotrexate within 2 weeks.

timeline.title: Focal RT for CNS Lesions
timeline.body: 30 Gy in 10 fractions or similar palliative regimen. For focal deficits from brain, spine, or cranial nerve disease. Part of multimodality approach.
